Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I'm juggling so much at 26. I tried to dedicate about 24 hours a week, usually in chunks between work and other responsibilities. I'm a visual learner, so I'd often create mind maps and diagrams from the lecture content. Some weeks I'd have a solid schedule, other weeks life just got in the way and I had to adapt on the fly.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was happening in my life. I aimed for consistency but with six online courses, deadlines would pile up and I'd have to shift priorities constantly. Some weeks I'd focus heavily on one course, then scramble to catch up on others. I learned to be flexible rather than rigid because that's just the reality of my situation.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I needed to check announcements or see if there were new discussion posts to respond to. I was pretty active in the forums, so I'd check in to see what my peers were saying. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch videos or download materials for later. Deadlines would also trigger me to log in more frequently to make sure I wasn't missing anything important.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the discussion boards became almost second nature to me. I found that engaging with my peers helped me process the material better, so I made it a habit to log in and contribute. It became part of my morning routine to check for new posts while having coffee. The social aspect of learning really kept me coming back regularly.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm such a visual learner. I'd watch them multiple times if needed, pausing to take visual notes. The discussion forums were also incredibly valuable because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click for me. I spent over 75 minutes on average with videos because that format just works for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete many of the written assignments - my completion rate was really low. I often felt overwhelmed trying to balance everything, so I'd prioritize understanding the material over formal submissions. Some of the text-heavy readings I'd skim rather than deeply engage with because they didn't match my learning style. I know that hurt my grades, but I had to make tough choices about where to put my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to the bigger picture or when it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. Visual content, interactive discussions, and anything that let me engage with peers felt valuable. If something felt like busywork or didn't seem to build toward real understanding, I'd deprioritize it even if I knew I shouldn't.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford with my limited time. Required ones created pressure that sometimes felt overwhelming, especially when multiple courses had deadlines at once. I actually engaged more authentically with optional discussions because there was no grade pressure - I could just learn and share ideas. Required assignments often felt more stressful even when the content was similar.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs to study during my commute or when I didn't have reliable internet. I created physical flashcards and visual diagrams that I'd review constantly. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources to supplement what we were learning, especially when I needed concepts explained in different ways.
Q10: So many times! I'd spend hours with printed materials, creating color-coded notes and diagrams at my kitchen table. I'd discuss concepts with friends who weren't even in my courses just to test my understanding. A lot of my exam preparation happened with materials I'd already downloaded, so the LMS wouldn't show me actively engaged even though I was studying intensely. My exam score of 69 reflects that learning even if my assignment completion didn't.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then work with it extensively offline. With six courses, I couldn't always be online, so I built a library of materials on my laptop. I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times, pause them, take notes, and create visual summaries - none of which would show up in the system.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my stress levels spiked and everything felt harder. Around midterms, I was dealing with personal stuff and my attendance dropped significantly. The moderate anxiety I carry made it hard to even open the LMS some days because I felt so behind. But I've learned to push through these moments - my resilience has been tested before and I know I can bounce back.
Q13: With six online courses, the workload was genuinely overwhelming at times. Fatigue was real - there were nights I'd log in, stare at the screen, and just not have the mental energy to engage meaningfully. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly. I'd sometimes just download what I needed and log off quickly because I couldn't afford to spend time navigating the platform.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other deadlines or life stuff. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what people were saying. Sometimes I'd log in with good intentions but feel so overwhelmed that I'd just click around without actually learning anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important - my 2.5% completion rate shows that clearly. The anxiety would build up around them, and I'd tell myself I'd do them later when I felt more prepared. Sometimes the gap between where I was and where I needed to be felt too big to bridge, so I'd focus on understanding the material instead of the formal submissions. It's something I'm still working on.
Q16: Deadlines created intense bursts of activity for me. I'd suddenly log in multiple times a day as a deadline approached, trying to gather everything I needed. They motivated me to engage but also increased my stress significantly. I often wished I could spread my effort more evenly, but the pressure of deadlines was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my procrastination.
Q17: When I did submit work and got feedback, it definitely influenced how I approached the next task. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. Critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me hesitant to submit the next thing. The CBT session I attended helped me reframe feedback as helpful rather than threatening, which made a difference.
Q18: The course structure with lots of discussion opportunities really worked for me since I thrive on peer interaction. Having video content available was essential for my visual learning style. However, the sheer volume of material across six courses made it hard to keep up with everything. I appreciated flexibility in when I could access materials, but I sometimes wished for more guidance on prioritizing.
Q19: The visual content and active discussion forums made engagement easier - those aligned with how I learn best. What made it harder was when there was too much text-heavy content without visual aids. The platform itself was fine, but sometimes finding specific resources felt cumbersome when I was in a hurry. Clearer pathways through the material would have helped me manage my limited time better.
Q20: I can totally relate to this pattern. Sometimes you're genuinely trying to learn and engage - watching videos, reading discussions, exploring resources - but the actual assignments feel like a separate mountain to climb. For me, understanding the material and completing formal work required different types of energy. High activity might show genuine learning effort even when submission rates are low.
Q21: This also makes sense to me. Someone might download everything early, study extensively offline, and only log in occasionally to check for updates. Their exam performance would reflect their actual learning even if their LMS activity looks minimal. I think I'm somewhere in between - I had high engagement in discussions but low assignment completion, yet I still passed with decent exam scores.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people access materials and how they interact with discussion forums. They capture video watch time and login frequency pretty accurately. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just clicking through, and the emotional experience of learning. They can't tell if someone was genuinely absorbing content or just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely curious about the material and actively trying to make sense of it. It's when I'm connecting new ideas to what I already know, discussing concepts with peers, and feeling invested in my own learning. It's not just about completing tasks - it's about that mental state where you're truly present and thinking deeply. Sometimes my best engagement happened when I wasn't even logged into anything.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed diagrams spread across my floor, or the conversations with friends where I explained concepts to test my understanding. It wouldn't capture the emotional journey - the anxiety before logging in, the satisfaction after a good discussion, or the resilience it took to keep going when I felt behind. It also wouldn't show how the CBT session helped me manage my stress and stay committed to my courses despite everything.
